# Preface {.unnumbered}

In this book I examine the foundations of behavioural economics.

The book is based on an undergraduate subject I teach in as part of UTS’s Bachelor of Economics program, [23005 Behavioural Economics](https://handbook.uts.edu.au/subjects/23005.html).

There are no mathematical prerequisites for this subject. Hence, the mathematics is kept at a basic level.

This book takes a traditional approach to behavioural economics, providing the basic economics foundations and examining how modifications to the core economic axioms lead to new insight. There is ample critique of the field (as you can read [here](https://www.jasoncollins.blog/a-critical-behavioural-economics-and-behavioural-science-reading-list/)), but this book is not primarily about that critique. Those foundations can be the launchpad for a critique, and without understanding those foundations the critique can be hollow.

This book covers the following areas:

- **Economic foundations**: In this part I introduce core economic concepts that we will use and modify in exploring behavioural economics.
- **Decision making under risk and uncertainty**: In this part I introduce the traditional economic approach to decision making under risk and uncertainty, and provide some empirical anomalies that arise when using this approach.
- **Prospect theory**: In this part I describe the pre-eminent alternative to expected utility theory, prospect theory. I also provide examples and possible applications.
- **Intertemporal choice**: In this part I explore decision making involving costs and benefits occurring at different times. I look at two types of time preference: exponential discounting and quasi-hyperbolic discounting.
- **Probability judgment**: In this part I introduce some core concepts of probability judgment and then examine the empirical evidence on how humans do not adhere to these foundational principles.
- **Game theory**: In this part I describe the game theoretic concepts that we use in our analysis of behavioural game theory and social preferences.
- **Behavioural game theory**: In this part I examine how heterogeneous agents and bounded rationality can change our analysis of economic games.
- **Social preferences**: In this part I consider how agents might incorporate concerns about the outcomes and beliefs of others into their decisions.
